speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 13. We're interviewing Dr. William Perl. I had asked you about whether, in your opinion, justice was served with the war crimes in Nuremberg and in the other trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only very partially of the ten thousands of responsible ones, a few only were tried. And very few were executed. In [INAUDIBLE], for example, you had 72 people tried, of whom 40 were convicted to hang, sentenced to hang. And not one of them was hung. And so I do think from this point of view, justice was not done. Justice was also not done because only the Germans were accused. There were not only Germans guilty. There were Lithuanians and Ukrainians. And some of the Baltic countries involved. But most of all, justice was not done. Because those who worked for the Holocaust in the State Department were not accused. When the Germans invaded Austria, the situation was horrible there. People committed suicides by the hundreds. I don't want to go into the detail. But the State Department then decided to call a conference, supposedly to help those people. And I would like to be permitted to read it. No, I can't. OK. So the assistant secretary of state, Mr. Welles-- the Messersmith, who was-- I don't know his third one now it is. Three people were responsible for altogether, the American foreign policy and specifically for Europe-- decided to call a conference for refugees-- if you were in France-- supposedly with the purpose of helping them. But actually, they admitted that this is available. And I read about this now, declassified. It was originally secret document. They wanted to counteract the tendency of America to help the refugees, to let them come in and to organize in the other countries that they should get in, to organize them by getting in front of the movement. And sending it towards nothing to happen. And that's what they did. They went to the conference with the intention to destroy the conference. This is quite clear. This is not my opinion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans, they didn't want to kill the Jews either at the beginning. There was a reich, but they thought it is not possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when they saw that America does not do anything after the Evian Conference, they decided-- there are documents for this that say-- we will have to do it themselves. And a document which I found in the German archives says, as far as the direction goes, and the possibility the world does not want the Jews. And the German delegation was sent to Evian to listen in to what's happened. Since they came back, said the reports says they don't want to help. So they will get nowhere. Therefore, we don't want to speak about the direction. And the conclusion of this is the German State Department, in the [INAUDIBLE] was still the world does not want the Jews. We have to tell the state themselves the direction and the means by which this should be achieved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is a direct connection between this. And Hitler still hesitated. And he wanted to get rid of the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he organized, for this reason, the pogrom of the 9th of November, 1938, which is formally called Kristallnacht. That's the name which the Germans give it because of the broken glass. There were many things committed which were incomparably worse than broken glass. And this national pogrom was organized with the-- this is fully documented-- purpose to show what will happen to the Jews if they do not emigrate, if the country will not let them emigrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to add before we conclude?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message that you would like to give to your children, grandchildren, or anybody else who'll be watching this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very important question. I didn't think about it in advance. I would've liked to know in advance. I'd give it a better answer. But all I can say now is that we have the duty to make the people aware, to make the world aware, of what they did to the Jews. Because it's easily forgotten. But really, in America, what is 50 years ago is forgotten. Doesn't count anymore. That's old stuff. And make them clearly aware what's happened. And to where the human spirit can be led to if we permit it to gain a hold and do not interrupt and do not step up against it right from the beginning. If we would have stepped-- the world would have stepped, stood out right from the beginning against it, it wouldn't have been. It would have been very bad. But mass extermination was nothing that was planned. That's all. And our children should know about it and should continue to tell this message. I feel very much obliged to those who were murdered, to carry the message and go on with this and tell it about and read, write about it again and again. Because it must never be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you for doing an interview with us today. And I'm sorry. I should have reminded you that I was going to ask that question. When we originally did the interview last month, I did my question. I should've reminded you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have prepared you a better answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you. It has been an honor to do the interview with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. The pleasure is to work with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Perl, would you please introduce this lovely lady sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the greatest happiness I should ever experience. She's the source of most of my happiness. Since I met her, I've been in love with her. I don't think there was a single week where I wasn't in love with her. And I'm thinking today, we're growing so much older. And love in a different way. But I don't only love her. I'm in love with her. That's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you like to tell us her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Lore, L-O-R-E. And it's not only the love. My respect is part of it. I respect her very much, for her strength, for her courage, for what she is. And I'm grateful to her that she is for what she is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So? That's enough?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Dr. Perl, can you please introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well, this is my wife, Lore. And she's not only my wife. She's my life. And these are my two sons, Rafael-- older one, who is a lawyer, working for the government. And the younger one is Solomon Michael, a veterinarian. And these are two grandchildren of mine, two out of the four. They're both the children of Rafael. This is Gili Perl, and she's six. And this is Ori Perl, and he's four. They're not present here because they couldn't make it is Solomon's wife, Darcy Perl, and Solomon's and Darcy's two children, two lovely children. One is a boy with the name of Jonathan Elliott. And the girl is about two months old, with the name of Juliana Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anybody like to say a few words now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to say, that it's very nice to be here together, kind of as a family, with three generations. But we do that quite often. As often as possible. Many people died and they didn't have that opportunity. And I'm very glad that we do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm very glad that out of the one Perl, there are 10 Perls today.

speaker: interviewee
text: For now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like that say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love being on television.

speaker: interviewee
text: I like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Dr. Perl, who is this in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, Rudolf Perl. It was taken in Vienna in about 1935, when he was 60 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Perl, who is this in this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother, Camilla Perl. She was born in Prague in 1885. And this picture was taken in Vienna, in 1938, shortly before the Nazis came to power. She was already a widow by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Perl, can you tell me about this document entitled 'The Council for German Jewry'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I came to England, after I got out from Vienna, I went to London and tried to get the support of the official Jewish organizations. The Council of German Jewry was the representative of all the refugee organizations, and so announced-- it says there-- that they are against the illegal immigration. Because immigration has to be properly prepared. And everything has to be audited against illegal immigration. And of course, there's many difficulties. This is a letter from the Czechoslovakian embassy in Romania, through the Romanian government, in which they ask to grant entrance visa, the free passage through Romania for a boat which will be waiting for the refugees in Sulina. And so it says that I am the representative of this organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what this is, Dr. Perl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a document which shows how the various agencies of the British government cooperated and were all mobilized against our undertaking. In this document, the British embassy in Bucharest informs the foreign minister of Britain that it intervenes. Which the Romanian government tried, then, to stop our activities. And that they already achieved the deportation of Dr. Willy Perl, who is an agent for this organization. This says here, he was involved diplomatics of it, the navy, and the intelligence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Perl, can you tell me what this document is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a document which is taken from a photocopy of an original paper from the British public records office in London. And it shows how active the British tried, and with antiquated methods, they tried to fight our efforts to save the money for visas. But having councils appointed ourselves everywhere-- in Budapest and in Paris, and in Vienna and in Berlin. And this reports about the intervention. Although they took the government of Palestine into it, and wrote this information of Palestine, they wanted to have a complete story before they intervened in Monrovia. And in the meanwhile, all our ships went, always there, with people, with Liberian and other visas, which were not genuine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Perl, can you tell me about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a letter from the Liberian Legation in Paris. The bureau has, actually, a very few diplomatic representatives in Europe. This is one from the Liberian Legation in Paris. And this asks for information-- where these visas were given, and under what circumstances. They wanted information in order to forward it then to Monrovia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a letter from Mr. Downey, the British Foreign Office, the one who was in charge of the area in which Palestine was located, in which he reports that he has learned of these false Liberian visas. And he has written to Palestine, to find out more about it. And the British government of Palestine didn't send him complete information. So they lost time again. In the meanwhile, again, every week a ship went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Perl, can you tell me what this document is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is the contract by which we charted the ships-- actually, the carrier-- from the Turkish owners. And arrangements according to which we had obliged ourselves, to make more trips with them, and only with them. And of course, we were ready to sign everything just to get the ships there. And the Turks were eager to get as much in advance promises as possible. There was a very big difficulty, by the way, in communicating with them. Because nobody spoke Turkish. But all the women there, one of the others had a girlfriend, a German girlfriend. Lila was her name. And she was very helpful. She was very friendly to us, and was very helpful, and tried to tell us that he wouldn't like this. Tell this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is one of the people on the train which went through Romania into the port, where they were trains shipped to the seagoing vessel. And sometimes, not always, the Romanians put wire around the windows so that nobody could run away and stay in Romania. I show this picture to several people. And they thought this is from people deported to Auschwitz. To Auschwitz you were not deported where you had so much space, as indicated here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Perl, can you tell me, what is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of one of the early deportations in Israel. After the people arrived, they were brought very near the coast. Then small boats came out and brought them as near to the shore that they finally could wade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been late in '38, or early 1939. Because we had more better ships then than these very little small ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Perl, can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another picture of another disembarking at the time when we used, already, larger ships to come close to the shore. Again, the last part had to be waded. And sometimes the waves were stronger than we expected. And people had to swim. And people who couldn't swim were taken on their backs by other people and brought to the beach. It was never a large distance. This is a photo of the which beached herself on the beach of Tel Aviv, right in the center of the city. She was prevented by the British, three times, to land. And finally, the captain decided to break through. And he was hunted by the British cannon boats. And he went through and beached himself right on the beach. And here this is being debarked. And of course, the authorities knew about it. I think everyone was arrested and brought to a camp where they, after some time, were released. And this is a picture here, of the same picture as seen from the beach. Because the population of Tel Aviv came with enthusiasm to look at this ship which had broken through the British blockade. And from here, you see them enthusiastically watching and discussing first that the ship. It's the same boat. But it's not being debarked yet. It is earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photo, Dr. Perl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After they were  arrested by the British on arrival, the people were brought to retention camp. In Atlit, usually. This is north of Haifa. And here they were, again, behind barbed wire. But it was another barbed wire than the barbed wire at home. And you see, in their faces, the happiness that they are here and that they are behind this barbed wire. They didn't care about wire. They were at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this document that's entitled 'Report to the Secretary on the Acquiescence of This Government in the Murder of the Jews'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Three high-ranking employees, lawyers or the top lawyers in the Interior Department who knew of what was going on-- how they informed the conference not to give visas-- got together and none of them were Jewish. They were all Protestants. And they find a memorandum of 18 pages to the Secretary of the Interior, in which they accuse the government to acquiesce in the murder of Jews by the Nazis. And this is the title. And then they go into details how they did it, how the main government is cooperating with the Nazis and killing the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what this is, Dr. Perl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a reward by the state of California, in which I was named as having saved thousands of Jews by my action. And they express, for this, their gratitude and their appreciation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this, Dr. Perl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is an award by the Simon Wiesenthal Center in Los Angeles, in which I was given the Distinguished Service Award for [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] 'decades of service to the Jewish people and for humanity; for his unflinching bravery in helping 40,000 Jews thwart the Nazis' plan for their destruction, for helping sow the seeds of national rebirth in the land of Israel, in the shadow of the greatest catastrophe to befall the Jewish people.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Perl, can you tell me about this letter from the White House?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a letter of which I'm very proud. It is a letter by President Reagan, thanking me for what I have done for humanity and for saving ten thousands of people. And it is worded in such a way that it is really a pleasure to read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a clipping from in which they report that I received high Austrian decoration, the Austrian Golden sign of achievement for what I did, saving Austrian Jews. I first refused to offer for such honor. Because Dr. Waldheim was then the president of Austria at that time. But I got it then. So I refused it to Austria. I got it from the Mayor of Vienna. Actually, it was from the Mayor of Vienna. He offered it to me, and I accepted it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a booklet which I wrote or put together in a very short form, a photographic report of our action prepared for the use of high schools in a very simple way, and showing the development of what we have done. And this is together, with a man who was in charge of the photographic work. His name is Fred Cherny, C-H-E-R-N-Y. This is not-- I will go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see, here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will not show all the pages. The story of the the ship, which brought Jews from Germany to Cuba. Cuba then receives visas from the government of Cuba, absolutely and fully right. And when they arrived in Cuba, it was shortly before the outbreak of the war. The Cubans didn't let them in. And they kept them on this German liner. Waited for, I believe, three weeks in the port. In the meanwhile, they contacted all the government of America to admit them. And nobody will admit them. And to the United States, even then, when the boats came back, they kept them very close to the coastline of Florida, where there were many Jews, hoping that some action would occur. And then, the Coast Guard was sent out to watch that nobody jumps the ship and goes to the shore. The ship came back to Germany through Europe. And sad to say that most of them perished. This book, was published by Crown Publishers, and describes the developing action saving-- rescue action including up to the time that it was not possible to travel through the Mediterranean anymore. Because the terrain had become a theater of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this book? This book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This book, is essentially the same as with an additional chapter and some changes. In the inside, they [INAUDIBLE] this edit. And it is, essentially, the second and the third edition of It was published first in hardcover, and then in paperback.

speaker: interviewer
text: This book?

speaker: interviewee
text: This book is one with which I was praying on for 10 years. I wanted to write it all the time. But it asked for very much research. And it was published in 1980 or '81. I predicted, I knew, already, what now the newspapers bring as big news, that all the other countries cooperated, that everybody knew what was happening to the Jews. I prove and then I document that the United States State Department, the British government, and all of the other governments knew, essentially, what was going on. And that in spite of it, kept the doors, the gates of Europe blocked. I like very much the cover, this swastika with the flags of the various countries in it. And OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is one of your recent articles. Who are these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are both children of my younger son, Solomon Perl. And they show Jonathan, about 17 months at that time. And the newborn daughter, or granddaughter, Juliana.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a picture of you here with Mr. Spielberg. Can you tell me when this was taken?

words.text: tell me when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture of me and Mr. Spielberg, taken at the Simon Wiesenthal Center in Los Angeles, in 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. This concludes the interview.
